sources.title: Grade 8 - Term 1: The Industrial Revolution in Britain and Southern Africa from 1860
Key terms: The Industrial Revolution in Britain and Southern Africa from 1860
Lesson: The Industrial Revolution in Britain and Southern Africa from 1860
Timeline: Events and inventions during the Industrial Revolution
Activity: The Industrial Revolution in Britain and Southern Africa from 1860
The Industrial Revolution and colonial expansion
Grade 8 - Topic 2 - The Mineral Revolution in South Africa
Some aspects of the South African industrial revolution
Migratory labour on the gold mines
Diamonds are first discovered at Kimberley, South Africa (the Big Hole)
De Beers Consolidated Mines Ltd. is founded to control diamond production

slides.heading: The Industrial Revolution in Britain and southern Africa
slides.lead: How steam, factories and cities changed Britain - and how that wave of change reached southern Africa.

image.alt: A factory with tall smoking chimneys beside a steam locomotive, under a smoky orange sky.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#efe7d8'/><rect y='128' width='320' height='52' fill='#8c7a5c'/><rect x='40' y='70' width='90' height='58' fill='#5a4a37'/><rect x='58' y='30' width='16' height='40' fill='#3f342a'/><rect x='92' y='38' width='16' height='32' fill='#3f342a'/><circle cx='66' cy='24' r='12' fill='#cfc3ad' opacity='0.8'/><circle cx='100' cy='30' r='10' fill='#cfc3ad' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='170' y='96' width='96' height='32' rx='5' fill='#7a1f1f'/><rect x='176' y='78' width='30' height='20' fill='#5c1414'/><circle cx='188' cy='132' r='12' fill='#2f2a24'/><circle cx='248' cy='132' r='12' fill='#2f2a24'/><rect x='150' y='124' width='120' height='6' fill='#4a4033'/><circle cx='262' cy='46' r='24' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking learners what machines and comforts they use every day that need factories, electricity or fuel to exist. Explain the two halves of this topic: first the Industrial Revolution that began in Britain, then how the demand for raw materials, markets and labour pulled southern Africa into the same industrial world - above all through diamonds and gold. Define 'revolution' as a very fast, deep change in how people live and work.

slides.heading: What was the Industrial Revolution?
slides.lead: From about the 1760s, Britain changed from making goods by hand to making them with machines in factories.

blocks.text: The **Industrial Revolution** was the period, beginning in Britain in the mid-1700s, when new machines and new sources of power let people produce goods far faster and in far greater numbers than ever before. Work moved out of homes and small workshops and into large **factories**.

items: Before: most people farmed the land and made goods by hand at home.
After: many people worked machines in factories in towns and cities.
Britain led the way because it had coal, iron, money to invest and trade routes across its empire.

slides.notes: Stress continuity and change. People had always made cloth, tools and pottery - but by hand and in small amounts. The revolution was in the scale and the speed. Introduce the idea that Britain's head start rested partly on wealth and raw materials drawn from its overseas colonies, which becomes important when we reach southern Africa.

slides.heading: Steam power
slides.lead: The steam engine gave factories a powerful, reliable source of energy that did not depend on rivers or wind.

blocks.text: Early machines were driven by water wheels, so factories had to be built next to fast rivers. In the 1760s and 1770s the Scottish engineer **James Watt** greatly improved the **steam engine**, which burned coal to boil water and drive machinery. Now a factory could be built almost anywhere.

items: Steam engines pumped water out of deep coal and metal mines.
They powered spinning and weaving machines in textile mills.
Later they drove trains and ships, moving goods and people quickly.

image.alt: A steam engine boiler with a piston and a large turning flywheel, with steam rising.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#efe7d8'/><rect x='30' y='78' width='70' height='48' rx='6' fill='#6b3a2e'/><rect x='40' y='60' width='14' height='18' fill='#4a281f'/><circle cx='47' cy='52' r='9' fill='#cfc3ad' opacity='0.85'/><circle cx='58' cy='42' r='7' fill='#cfc3ad' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='100' y='96' width='36' height='10' fill='#7a5a3a'/><circle cx='168' cy='101' r='34' fill='none' stroke='#3f342a' stroke-width='8'/><circle cx='168' cy='101' r='6' fill='#3f342a'/><rect x='30' y='126' width='150' height='8' fill='#4a4033'/></svg>

slides.notes: Keep the science simple: heat water to steam, steam pushes a piston, the piston turns a wheel that drives machines. The key freedom Watt's engine gave was location - factories no longer had to sit on a river. Link forward: coal, the fuel that fed the steam engine, becomes one of the industries that grew fastest.

slides.heading: The factory system
slides.lead: Machines, workers and set hours were brought together under one roof.

blocks.heading: Machines
blocks.text: Big powered machines did in minutes what once took a worker hours by hand.

blocks.heading: The clock
blocks.text: Workers had to arrive on time and work long, fixed shifts set by the factory bell.

blocks.heading: Wages
blocks.text: People no longer made whole goods; they were paid a wage to do one small task all day.

slides.notes: Use these three cards to explain what was new about factory work: discipline by the clock, division of labour into small repeated tasks, and dependence on a wage rather than land or a craft. Ask learners to compare this with how work was organised before - at home, at the family's own pace. This sets up the human cost on the next slide.

slides.heading: Coal, iron and railways
slides.lead: New industries and new transport fed off each other and spread across Britain.

blocks.text: Steam engines needed **coal**, machines and rails were made of **iron** and later steel, and steam **railways** carried all of it quickly across the country. In 1830 the Liverpool and Manchester Railway opened, showing how fast people and goods could now travel.

items: Coal mining and iron making grew into huge industries.
Railways and canals linked mines, factories, towns and ports.
Steamships carried British goods - and brought in raw materials - across the empire.

image.alt: A steam train on rails pulling wagons of coal past a signal.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#efe7d8'/><rect y='118' width='240' height='42' fill='#8c7a5c'/><rect x='30' y='70' width='60' height='42' rx='5' fill='#7a1f1f'/><rect x='36' y='52' width='16' height='18' fill='#5c1414'/><circle cx='44' cy='44' r='9' fill='#cfc3ad' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='96' y='84' width='40' height='28' fill='#3f342a'/><rect x='142' y='84' width='40' height='28' fill='#3f342a'/><circle cx='46' cy='116' r='9' fill='#2f2a24'/><circle cx='78' cy='116' r='9' fill='#2f2a24'/><circle cx='112' cy='116' r='8' fill='#2f2a24'/><circle cx='158' cy='116' r='8' fill='#2f2a24'/><rect x='20' y='124' width='200' height='5' fill='#4a4033'/><rect x='206' y='70' width='6' height='42' fill='#5a4a37'/><circle cx='209' cy='72' r='6' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Show the chain reaction: steam needs coal, machines and rails need iron, railways carry both and open new markets. This is why historians call it a 'revolution' - one change triggered another. Highlight the empire link (steamships and trade) because it directly connects to why Britain expanded into places like southern Africa.

slides.heading: Urbanisation: the rise of cities
slides.lead: Factories drew huge numbers of people from the countryside into fast-growing towns.

blocks.text: **Urbanisation** means the growth of towns and cities as people move to them. As factories opened, families left farms to look for work, and cities such as Manchester and Birmingham grew very quickly. Housing, water and sanitation often could not keep up.

items: Workers crowded into cheap, cramped housing near the factories.
Overcrowding and poor sanitation spread disease.
For the first time, many countries had more people living in towns than on the land.

image.alt: Rows of narrow crowded houses with chimneys packed tightly together.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#efe7d8'/><rect y='126' width='240' height='34' fill='#8c7a5c'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><rect x='16' y='78' width='34' height='48'/><rect x='54' y='70' width='34' height='56'/><rect x='92' y='80' width='34' height='46'/><rect x='130' y='68' width='34' height='58'/><rect x='168' y='82' width='34' height='44'/><rect x='206' y='74' width='28' height='52'/></g><g fill='#3f342a'><rect x='24' y='60' width='7' height='18'/><rect x='62' y='52' width='7' height='18'/><rect x='138' y='50' width='7' height='18'/><rect x='176' y='64' width='7' height='18'/></g><g fill='#cdd6b5'><rect x='26' y='92' width='12' height='14'/><rect x='64' y='86' width='12' height='14'/><rect x='102' y='94' width='12' height='14'/><rect x='140' y='84' width='12' height='14'/><rect x='178' y='96' width='12' height='14'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Define urbanisation clearly because it is a key CAPS term and returns when we look at South Africa. Balance the picture: cities offered work and wages, but early industrial cities were overcrowded and unhealthy. Ask learners whether their own town or a nearby city grew because of jobs - connect the concept to their world.

slides.heading: Social change and working conditions
slides.lead: The revolution created great wealth for some and hard, dangerous work for many.

blocks.text: Factory owners and merchants grew rich, and a new **middle class** appeared. But many workers - including women and children - laboured long hours in dangerous factories and mines for low pay. Over time, workers formed **trade unions** and campaigned for laws to protect them.

items: Children worked in factories and mines, and many were injured. [SME] Confirm any specific ages or statistics before use.
Reformers won laws limiting working hours and child labour.
Society split more sharply into a wealthy owning class and a working class.

image.alt: A scale weighing a bag of coins against a worker figure, showing an imbalance.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#efe7d8'/><rect x='106' y='30' width='8' height='96' fill='#5a4a37'/><rect x='70' y='30' width='80' height='6' fill='#5a4a37'/><line x1='78' y1='36' x2='78' y2='70' stroke='#5a4a37' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='142' y1='36' x2='142' y2='58' stroke='#5a4a37' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='78' cy='80' r='16' fill='#e8a13a'/><text x='78' y='85' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' fill='#5c3a10'>R</text><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='142' cy='66' r='8'/><rect x='136' y='74' width='12' height='20'/></g><rect x='70' y='126' width='80' height='6' fill='#4a4033'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this responsibly: the point is that industrialisation brought progress and hardship at the same time. Emphasise cause and effect - poor conditions led workers to organise unions and push for reform laws. The [SME] flag marks the child-labour detail: keep it general unless a reviewer supplies verified ages or figures. This idea of a rich owning class and a working class transfers directly to the South African mines.

slides.heading: Inventions and change in Britain

blocks.alt: A timeline of key inventions of the Industrial Revolution from the 1760s to 1840.

events.label: James Watt patents his improved steam engine
First public steam railway (Stockton to Darlington)
Britain is the world's leading industrial power

slides.notes: Use this timeline to consolidate the Britain half of the topic before crossing to southern Africa. Ask learners to spot the pattern: textile machines first, then steam power, then railways carrying everything. Note that these dates are approximate turning points, not the only important events. [SME] A reviewer should confirm the exact dates against the SAHO inventions timeline before print.

slides.heading: Why the revolution reached southern Africa
slides.lead: Industrial Britain needed raw materials, new markets and cheap labour - and looked to its colonies.

blocks.text: Britain's factories needed **raw materials** such as cotton, wool and metals, and its merchants wanted **new markets** to sell finished goods. This drove Britain to expand its empire, including into southern Africa. When diamonds and then gold were discovered here, that pull became far stronger.

items: Industrial economies needed materials that colonies could supply.
British money and machines flowed in to mine and to build railways.
This is often called the **Mineral Revolution** in South Africa.

slides.notes: Bridge the two halves of the topic explicitly. The same forces that industrialised Britain - the hunger for raw materials, markets and profit - reshaped southern Africa. Introduce 'Mineral Revolution' as the local name for South Africa's rapid industrial change, driven by mining rather than textiles. This is the pivot point of the lesson.

slides.heading: Diamonds and the digging of Kimberley
slides.lead: The discovery of diamonds turned a quiet region into a booming mining town almost overnight.

blocks.text: From 1867 diamonds were found near the Orange and Vaal rivers, and a huge rush began around what became **Kimberley**. Thousands of diggers hollowed out the famous **Big Hole**. In 1888 **Cecil John Rhodes** brought the mines together under his company, **De Beers Consolidated Mines**, gaining control of most of the world's diamond supply.

items: Kimberley grew into one of the largest towns in southern Africa.
Small diggers were pushed out as rich companies took over.
Black workers were housed in closed compounds and paid far less than white workers.

image.alt: A deep terraced open mine pit shaped like a funnel, with tiny figures at the top.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#efe7d8'/><rect y='40' width='220' height='24' fill='#b7a17a'/><path d='M10 64 L210 64 L150 150 L70 150 Z' fill='#6b5236'/><path d='M28 64 L192 64 L140 132 L80 132 Z' fill='#7a5f40'/><path d='M48 64 L172 64 L128 112 L92 112 Z' fill='#8a6d49'/><path d='M70 64 L150 64 L118 96 L102 96 Z' fill='#5a4630'/><g fill='#3f342a'><rect x='40' y='54' width='4' height='10'/><rect x='96' y='54' width='4' height='10'/><rect x='160' y='54' width='4' height='10'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Kimberley is the first clear example of the Mineral Revolution. Two big ideas: the rush created a large industrial town in the interior, and control quickly passed from many small diggers to a few powerful companies led by Rhodes. Introduce the compound and migrant-labour system gently here; the next slide develops it. [SME] Confirm the Eureka (1867) versus Kimberley 'New Rush' (1871) framing if precise dates are needed.

slides.heading: Gold on the Witwatersrand
slides.lead: The discovery of gold in 1886 created Johannesburg and made South Africa central to the world economy.

blocks.text: In 1886 a major gold reef was found on the **Witwatersrand**, and the mining town of **Johannesburg** sprang up within months. The gold lay deep underground, so mining it needed expensive machinery, large companies and enormous numbers of workers.

items: Johannesburg became the fastest-growing city in southern Africa.
Deep-level mining needed big money, so mining houses grew very powerful.
Gold made South Africa important to Britain and to the whole world economy.

image.alt: A tall mine headgear tower with a winding wheel above a shaft, and gold ore below.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#efe7d8'/><rect y='130' width='200' height='30' fill='#8c7a5c'/><path d='M70 130 L86 40 L114 40 L130 130 Z' fill='none' stroke='#5a4a37' stroke-width='6'/><line x1='78' y1='90' x2='122' y2='90' stroke='#5a4a37' stroke-width='5'/><line x1='74' y1='60' x2='126' y2='60' stroke='#5a4a37' stroke-width='5'/><circle cx='100' cy='40' r='14' fill='none' stroke='#3f342a' stroke-width='5'/><rect x='150' y='108' width='16' height='12' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='158' y='96' width='16' height='12' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Contrast diamonds and gold. Diamonds could be picked from the surface at first; Witwatersrand gold was deep and low-grade, so from the start it needed heavy machinery, deep shafts and huge capital. That is why powerful mining companies and a massive labour force appeared so fast. Johannesburg is the clearest South African example of urbanisation driven by industry.

slides.heading: Migrant labour, compounds and new cities
slides.lead: The mines reshaped where and how southern Africans lived and worked.

blocks.text: The mines needed a huge, cheap workforce. Many black men travelled long distances from rural areas to work on the mines for months at a time - the **migrant labour** system - while their families stayed behind. Workers lived in crowded **compounds**, were paid low wages and had to carry **passes** that controlled their movement.

items: Taxes and loss of land pushed many rural men to seek mine work.
Racial divisions in pay and housing were built into the mining system.
This mix of migrant labour and racial control shaped South Africa long after 1900.

image.alt: A line of worker figures walking towards mine buildings, carrying bundles.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#efe7d8'/><rect y='124' width='240' height='36' fill='#8c7a5c'/><rect x='150' y='60' width='70' height='64' fill='#6b5236'/><rect x='160' y='74' width='16' height='16' fill='#cdd6b5'/><rect x='188' y='74' width='16' height='16' fill='#cdd6b5'/><g fill='#3f342a'><circle cx='34' cy='78' r='10'/><rect x='27' y='90' width='14' height='34'/><circle cx='70' cy='74' r='10'/><rect x='63' y='86' width='14' height='38'/><circle cx='106' cy='80' r='10'/><rect x='99' y='92' width='14' height='32'/></g><g fill='#7a5a3a'><rect x='22' y='70' width='10' height='10'/><rect x='60' y='66' width='10' height='10'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This is the most sensitive slide - present it factually and with care. The mineral economy did not just create wealth and cities; it created a system of migrant labour, compounds, low racial wages and pass controls that caused real hardship and split families. Make the link to the Britain half: just as British workers faced hard conditions and a divided society, South Africa's mining economy produced deep inequality - here organised along racial lines. This connects forward to later topics on segregation and apartheid.

slides.heading: Britain and southern Africa compared
slides.lead: The same forces of industry produced similar changes in two very different places.

items: Driven by steam, coal and textile factories.
Cities like Manchester grew fast.
A new middle class and a working class.
Driven by diamond and gold mining.
Cities like Kimberley and Johannesburg grew fast.
Wealthy mine owners and a migrant workforce divided by race.

slides.notes: Use this side-by-side slide to draw out the CAPS skill of comparison. Similarities: rapid industrial growth, fast-growing cities, sharp social divisions between owners and workers. Key difference: in South Africa those divisions were organised by race, and the workforce was largely migrant. Have learners suggest one similarity and one difference in their own words before you reveal the columns.

slides.heading: What we learned
slides.lead: Steam and factories transformed Britain; diamonds and gold pulled southern Africa into the same industrial world.

blocks.text: The Industrial Revolution moved work into machine-powered factories, filled cities with people, and split society into owners and workers. Britain's need for materials and markets, together with the discovery of **diamonds** and **gold**, brought this rapid industrial change to southern Africa in the form of the **Mineral Revolution**.

blocks.question: Explain how the steam engine changed the way goods were made in Britain.
Describe two ways the discovery of diamonds and gold changed life in southern Africa.
Give one way industrial change was similar in Britain and southern Africa, and one way it was different.

slides.notes: Close by returning to the title question: how did change in Britain reach southern Africa? Recap the chain - steam and factories, urbanisation, social division, then the same forces arriving here through mining. Use the three questions as a written task or discussion; the mark allocations follow CAPS source-based and extended-writing styles. Remind learners this topic sets up later work on colonial expansion, the South African War and segregation.
